spans.text: Sampling represents a continuous-time signal by taking values at regularly spaced time instants. The spacing is the sampling interval Ts and the rate is Fs = 1/Ts.
For a band-limited sinusoid with highest frequency B, faithful reconstruction requires a sampling rate greater than twice B. Sampling below this boundary can create aliasing.
For x(t)=cos(2πft), sampling at t=nTs gives x[n]=cos(2πf/Fs · n). The discrete sequence depends on the frequency ratio f/Fs.
Frequencies that differ by an integer multiple of Fs produce the same sampled sequence because cos(2π(f+kFs)/Fs · n)=cos(2πf/Fs · n) for integer n and k.
Aliasing cannot be repaired after sampling by simply looking at the samples; the sampling rate or an anti-aliasing filter must prevent the ambiguity before conversion.
With Fs=40 Hz, a 10 Hz sinusoid and a 50 Hz sinusoid differ by one sampling frequency and therefore produce the same discrete-time samples.
